question:
Assertion: In group 15 elements nitrogen does not form pentahalides.

Reason: Nitrogen can exhibit +5 oxidation state.
explanation:
Electronic configuration of nitrogen $(\mathrm{N})=1 \mathrm{~s}^{2}, 2 \mathrm{~s}^{2} 2 \mathrm{p}^{3}$. It has only s and p-orbitals.
It doesn't possess d -orbitals to expand its covalency beyond four. That is why it doesn't form pentahalides.
Nitrogen can show +5 oxidation state in compounds like $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$.
Hence, Both Assertion and Reason are true but Reason is not correct explanation of Assertion.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
Identify the correct orders from the following with respect to the property associated with
A. $\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}$ - Bond angle
B. $\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}$ - Basic character
C. $\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ - Thermal stability
D. $\mathrm{SbH}_{3}>\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}$ - Boiling point
explanation: Given A, B and D are correct orders and for given order C (thermal stability) is incorrect. The correct order is $\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}$.

question: The correct order of reducing ability of the following hydrides is
explanation: The reducing character of the hydrides of group 15 elements depends upon the stability of hydrides. With progressive decrease in stability, the reducing character of hydrides increases as we move down the group. Thus, ammonia being stable has least reducing ability. The order of reducing abilities of $15{ }^{\text {th }}$ group hydrides is   $\mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{PH}_{3} < \mathrm{AsH}_{3} < \mathrm{SbH}_{3} < \mathrm{BiH}_{3}$

question:
Choose the correct statements about the hydrides of group 15 elements.
(I) The stability of the hydrides decreases in the order ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}>\mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$.
(II) The reducing ability of the hydrides increases in the order ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{PH}_{3} < \mathrm{AsH}_{3} < \mathrm{SbH}_{3} < \mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$
(III) Among the hydrides, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ is strong reducing agent while ${\mathrm{\mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$ is mild reducing agent.
(IV) The basicity of the hydrides increases in the order ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{PH}_{3} < \mathrm{AsH}_{3} < \mathrm{SbH}_{3} < \mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$.
Choose the most appropriate from the options given below:
explanation: From ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ to ${\mathrm{\mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$, thermal stability decreases (as bond energy decreases), basicity decreases (as electronegativity decreases), reducing ability increases (as bond energy decreases). ${\mathrm{\mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$ is strong reducing agent and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ is mild. This is because as nitrogen being unable to easily accommodate a lone pair due to its small size while the larger atom can do so. So, the correct option is (4).

parent_topic: Chemical Properties and General Characteristics of Group – 15 Elements

order of reducing nature is

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option out of the options given below.

Assertion: ${\mathrm{\mathrm{PH}_{3}}}$ has lower boiling point than ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$.

Reason: In liquid state, ${\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ molecules are associated through van der Waal's forces,but ${\rm{P}}{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{3}}}$ molecules are associated through hydrogen bonding.
explanation:
In ammonia ${\mathrm{\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)}}$, the molecules are linked by intermolecular hydrogen bonding but such hydrogen bonding is not possible in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{PH}_{3}}}$.
So, boiling point of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ is higher than ${\mathrm{\mathrm{PH}_{3}}}$ and the correct option is (3).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

question:
How many of the following trends is(are) CORRECT with respect to the properties of hydrides of group 15 elements?
i. Basic strength:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3} \geq \mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$
ii. Reducing character:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{PH}_{3} < \mathrm{AsH}_{3} < \mathrm{SbH}_{3} < \mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$
iii. Thermal stability:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}>\mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$
iv. Boiling point:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{PH}_{3} < \mathrm{AsH}_{3} < \mathrm{SbH}_{3} < \mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$
explanation:
The boiling point of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{NH}_{3}}}$ is higher than that of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{PH}_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{AsH}_{3}}}$. This is due to its tendency to form hydrogen bonds. Hence, the trend in boiling point is
${\mathrm{\mathrm{PH}_{3} < \mathrm{AsH}_{3} < \mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{SbH}_{3} < \mathrm{BiH}_{3}}}$.

$\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{PH}_{3}, \mathrm{AsH}_{3}, \mathrm{SbH}_{3}, \mathrm{BiH}_{3}$
explanation: The basic strength of given hydrides decreases in the order as follows :$\mathrm{NH}_{3}>\mathrm{PH}_{3}>\mathrm{AsH}_{3}>\mathrm{SbH}_{3}>\mathrm{BiH}_{3}$This is due to the decrease in the availability of the lone pair of electrons which depends on the size of central atom that increases from $\mathrm{N}$ to $\mathrm{Bi}$. Therefore, $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{BiH}_{3}$ acts as a strong and weak base hydride respectively.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is less reactive than $\mathrm{P}_{4}$.


Reason: Nitrogen has more electron gain enthalpy than phosphorus.
explanation: $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{P}$ single bond in $\mathrm{P}_{4}$ molecules is much weaker $\left(213 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$ than $\mathrm{N} \equiv \mathrm{N}$ triple bond $\left(941.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$ in $\mathrm{N}_{2}$. So $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is less reactive than $\mathrm{P}_{4}$. Electron gain enthalpy of nitrogen is less than phosphorus. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Among nitrogen halides $\mathrm{NX}_{3}$, the dipole moment is highest for $\mathrm{NI}_{3}$ and lowest for $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$.


Reason: Nitrogen halides $\mathrm{NX}_{3}$, have trigonal pyramidal structure.
explanation:
$\mathrm{NI}_{3}$ has highest dipole moment due to the higher electronegativity of $\mathrm{N}$ than $\mathrm{I}$ atom. In $\mathrm{NF}_{3}$, the dipole moments due to lone pair and $\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{F}$ bonds is partially cancelled, as $\mathrm{F}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{N}$.
All $\mathrm{NX}_{3}$ have pyramidal shape as the nitrogen atom undergoes $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridisation with a lone pair. So option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Given below are two statements. choose the correct answer from the options given below:
: The boiling point of hydrides of Group 16 elements follow the order
${{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}\,{\rm{ > }}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Te}}\,{\rm{ > }}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{Se}}\,{\rm{ > }}\,{{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{\,S}}{\rm{.}}$
On the basis of molecular mass, ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ is expected to have lower boiling point than the other members of the group but due to the presence of extensive H -bonding in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$, it has higher boiling point.
explanation:
Boiling points of hydrides of group 16 follow the order ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}>\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Te}>\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{Se}>\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}}}$.
Due to intermolecular H -bonding ${\mathrm{\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}}}$ shows higher boiling point than respective hydrides of group 16 .
So both statements are true.

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$ cannot be hydrolysed but $\mathrm{SF}_{4}$ can be.


Reason: Six $\mathrm{F}$ atoms in $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$ prevent the attack of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ on sulphur atom of $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$.
explanation: $\mathrm{SF}_{6}$ resists hydrolysis due to its symmetrical and stable structure, preventing interaction with water. In contrast, $\mathrm{SF}_{4}$ can undergo hydrolysis as its trigonal bipyramidal shape allows interaction with water, leading to the breakdown into $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and HF. Therefore, option (1) is correct.

question:
Consider the following statements about the oxides of halogens.
A. At room temperature, $\mathrm{OF}_{2}$ is thermally stable.
B. Order of stability of oxides of halogens is $\mathrm{I}>\mathrm{Br}>\mathrm{Cl}$.

explanation:

(B) Order of stability of oxides of halogens is
I $>$ Cl $>$ Br $>$ F.
(C) Iodine pentoxide $\left[\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}\right]$ easily oxidises carbon monoxide to carbon dioxide at room temperature. $5 \mathrm{CO}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5} \rightarrow \mathrm{I}_{2}+5 \mathrm{CO}$

question:
Thiosulphate reacts differently with iodine and bromine in the reactions given below:
${\mathrm{2 \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}+\mathrm{I}_{2} \rightarrow \mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}+2 \mathrm{I}^{-}}}$
${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}+5 \mathrm{Br}_{2}+5 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}+10 \mathrm{Br}^{-}+10 \mathrm{H}^{+}}}$
Which of the following statement justifies the above dual behaviour of thiosulphate?
explanation:
${\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}_{2}}}$ oxidises ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}(+2 \,\mathrm{O} . \mathrm{S})}}$ to
${\mathrm{\mathrm{SO}_{4}^{2-}(+6}}$ O.S. ${\mathrm{)}}$ while ${\mathrm{\mathrm{I}_{2}}}$ oxidises
${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}^{2-}(+2}}$ O.S ${\mathrm{)}}$ to ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{4} \mathrm{O}_{6}^{2-}(+2.5}}$ O.S. ${\mathrm{)}}$,
hence ${\mathrm{\mathrm{Br}_{2}}}$ is stronger oxidant than iodine. So, the correct option is (4).

options.text: Bromine is a weaker oxidant than iodine.
Thiosulphate undergoes oxidation by bromine and reduction by iodine in these reactions.
Bromine undergoes oxidation and iodine undergoes reduction in these reactions.
Bromine is a stronger oxidant than iodine.

question: Extraction of chlorine from brine solution is based on
explanation:
Chlorine can be manufactured by
electrolysis of a sodium chloride solution (brine).
Electrolysis of brine solution involves the following reactions:
At cathode : $2 \mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \quad \text { (Reduction) }$
At anode :
$2 \mathrm{Cl}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Cl}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} \quad \text { (Oxidation) }$
Overall reaction:
$2{\text{NaC}}{{\text{l}}_{({\text{aq}})}} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{{\text{O}}_{(1)}} \to $ ${\text{C}}{{\text{l}}_{2({\text{g}})}} + {{\text{H}}_{2({\text{g}})}} + 2{\text{NaO}}{{\text{H}}_{({\text{aq}})}}$

$3 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}+6 \mathrm{NaOH}_{\text {(conc) }} \xrightarrow{\text { heat }}$
$5 \mathrm{NaCl}+\mathrm{NaClO}_{3}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Chlorine is simultaneously reduced to

question:
Explain why the stability of oxoacids of chlorine increases in the order given below :
$\mathrm{HClO} < \mathrm{HClO}_{2} < \mathrm{HClO}_{3} < \mathrm{HClO}_{4}$
explanation:
Oxygen is more electronegative than chlorine, therefore dispersal of negative charge present on chlorine increases from $\mathrm{ClO}^{-}$to $\mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-}$ ion because number of oxygen atoms attached to chlorine is increasing. Therefore, stability of ions will increase in the order given below:
$\mathrm{ClO}^{-} < \mathrm{ClO}_{2}^{-} < \mathrm{ClO}_{3}^{-} < \mathrm{ClO}_{4}^{-}$
Thus, due to increase in stability of conjugate base, acidic strength of corresponding acid increases in the following order:
$\mathrm{HClO} < \mathrm{HClO}_{2} < \mathrm{HClO}_{3} < \mathrm{HClO}_{4}$

options.text: Dispersal of negative charge on Chlorine increases with number of oxygens
Oxygen forms double bonds with chlorine

question:
$\underset{(1: 20)}{\mathrm{Xe}(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{F}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow[60-70 \text { bar }]{573 \mathrm{~K}} \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{s})$
$\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Y}+\mathrm{HF}, \mathrm{X}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Z}+\mathrm{HF}$
The correct statements regarding Y and Z are
(I) Y has square pyramidal geometry.
(II) Y has linear geometry.
(III) Z has $3 \sigma, 3 \pi$ bonds and 1 lone pair of electrons on the central atom.
(IV) Z has tetrahedral geometry.
explanation:
$\underset{\text { (1:20) }}{\mathrm{Xe}(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{F}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow[60-70 \text { bar }]{573 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\substack{\text { (X) } \\ \text { (Distorted } \\ \text { octahedral) }}}{\mathrm{XeF}_{6}(\mathrm{~s})}$
$\underset{(\mathrm{X})}{\mathrm{XeF}_{6}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{(\text { Y) } \\ \text { (Square } \\ \text { pyramidal) }}}{\mathrm{XeOF}_{4}}+\mathrm{HF}$
$\mathrm{XeF}_{6}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { (Z) } \\ \text { (Trigonal } \\ \text { pyramidal) }}}{\mathrm{XeO}_{3}}+6 \mathrm{HF}$
Thus, statements II and IV are incorrect while statement I is correct. Also, from the structure of compound Z i.e., $\mathrm{XeO}_{3}$ it is clear that statement III is also correct
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958083/preptracker/questions/q_107219_explanation_1764958082521.png i.e., it has $3 \sigma$ bonds, $3 \pi$ - bonds and 1 lone pair of electrons.

question:
The correct decreasing order of the following 'Xe' compounds to act as both fluorinating and oxidising agent is
(i) $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$
(ii) $\mathrm{XeF}_{4}$
(iii) $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$
explanation:
The oxidation state of central atom, Xe increases from $ + 2$ to $ + 4$ to $ + 6$ in $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}, \mathrm{XeF}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$ respectively. Thus, its tendency to accept electrons also increases and thus, its oxidising power increases.
Also, $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$ can give more fluorine than $\mathrm{XeF}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$. So, the fluorinating agent order is $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}>\mathrm{XeF}_{4}>\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$.
Hence, the correct decreasing order of the given 'Xe' compound to act as both fluorinating and oxidising agent is $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}>\mathrm{XeF}_{4}>\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation and Properties of Xenon – Fluorine Compounds

question: Xenon hexafluoride reacts with silica to form a xenon compound ${\rm{X}}$. The oxidation state of xenon in ${\rm{X}}$ is
explanation:
Xenon hexafluoride reacts with silica to form $\mathrm{XeOF}_{4}$ as
$2 \mathrm{XeF}_{6}+\mathrm{SiO}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{XeOF}_{4}+\mathrm{SiF}_{4}$
The oxidation state of xenon in $\mathrm{XeOF}_{4}$ is +6 as calculated below:
Let the oxidation state of $\mathrm{Xe}$ is $a$.
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad & a+1 \times(-2)+4 \times(-1)=0 \\& a-2-4=0 \quad a=+6\end{aligned}$

question:
Assertion: Helium is used in diving apparatus.


Reason: Solubility of helium is less in blood.
explanation: In diving apparatus, a blend of oxygen and helium is employed to achieve an equivalent partial pressure of oxygen as that found in normal air at sea level $(1 \mathrm{~atm})$. Nitrogen has increased solubility in blood and can lead to the formation of bubbles, causing decompression sickness or "the bends." To mitigate this risk, nitrogen is substituted with helium, as it is significantly less soluble in biological fluids, reducing the likelihood of bubble formation during decompression. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Uses of Inert Gases and Its Compounds

question:
Assertion: Xenon forms fluorides.


Reason: Because 5d- orbitals are available for valence shell expansion.
explanation: Xenon forms fluorides primarily because only fluorine and oxygen are electronegative enough to excite electrons of Xenon into its vacant 5d-orbitals and allow bonding. So option (1) is correct.

Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: Arrange the hydrides $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{HF}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{HCl}$ in the increasing order of their boiling points
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{HF}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ have hydrogen bonding as the intermolecular forces while HCl has weaker dipole dipole interaction.
Thus, HCl has the lowest boiling point. Among $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{HF}$, and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has the highest boiling point due to extensive network of hydrogen bonds. Among $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and HF, HF forms stronger hydrogen bonds due to very high electronegativity of fluorine. Thus, the order of boiling point will be : $\mathrm{HCl} < \mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{HF} < \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
subject: Chemistry

explanation:
Complete hydrolysis of $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$ produces xenon trioxide.
${\text{Xe}}{{\text{F}}_6} + 3{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {\text{Xe}}{{\text{O}}_3} + 6{\text{HF}}$
Whereas partial hydrolysis of $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$ form different products.
${\text{Xe}}{{\text{F}}_6} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {\text{Xe}}{{\text{O}}_2}\,{{\text{F}}_2} + 4{\text{HF}}$
${\text{Xe}}{{\text{F}}_6} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {\text{XeO}}{{\text{F}}_4} + 2{\text{HF}}$

question:
$\underset{(1: 20)}{\mathrm{Xe}(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{F}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow[60-70 \text { bar }]{573 \mathrm{~K}} \mathrm{X}(\mathrm{s})$
$\mathrm{X}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Y}+\mathrm{HF}, \mathrm{X}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \mathrm{Z}+\mathrm{HF}$
The correct statements regarding Y and Z are
(I) Y has square pyramidal geometry.
(II) Y has linear geometry.
(III) Z has $3 \sigma, 3 \pi$ bonds and 1 lone pair of electrons on the central atom.
(IV) Z has tetrahedral geometry.
explanation:
$\underset{\text { (1:20) }}{\mathrm{Xe}(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{F}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \xrightarrow[60-70 \text { bar }]{573 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\substack{\text { (X) } \\ \text { (Distorted } \\ \text { octahedral) }}}{\mathrm{XeF}_{6}(\mathrm{~s})}$
$\underset{(\mathrm{X})}{\mathrm{XeF}_{6}}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{(\text { Y) } \\ \text { (Square } \\ \text { pyramidal) }}}{\mathrm{XeOF}_{4}}+\mathrm{HF}$
$\mathrm{XeF}_{6}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \rightarrow \underset{\substack{\text { (Z) } \\ \text { (Trigonal } \\ \text { pyramidal) }}}{\mathrm{XeO}_{3}}+6 \mathrm{HF}$
Thus, statements II and IV are incorrect while statement I is correct. Also, from the structure of compound Z i.e., $\mathrm{XeO}_{3}$ it is clear that statement III is also correct
https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764958083/preptracker/questions/q_107219_explanation_1764958082521.png i.e., it has $3 \sigma$ bonds, $3 \pi$ - bonds and 1 lone pair of electrons.

question:
Catalysts in the following reactions are
(I)$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOCH}_{3}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq})$
(II) $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
(III) $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
(IV) $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation:
I. Esterification reaction is catalysed by mineral acid such as $\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{l})$.
II. Conversion of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ into $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ is catalysed by $\operatorname{Pt}(\mathrm{s})$.
III. In lead chamber process conversion of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ into $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ by reaction with $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is catalysed by $\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$.
IV. In Haber's process ammonia is manufactured by reacting $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ in presence of $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})$.

question:
The correct decreasing order of the following 'Xe' compounds to act as both fluorinating and oxidising agent is
(i) $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$
(ii) $\mathrm{XeF}_{4}$
(iii) $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$
explanation:
The oxidation state of central atom, Xe increases from $ + 2$ to $ + 4$ to $ + 6$ in $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}, \mathrm{XeF}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$ respectively. Thus, its tendency to accept electrons also increases and thus, its oxidising power increases.
Also, $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}$ can give more fluorine than $\mathrm{XeF}_{4}$ and $\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$. So, the fluorinating agent order is $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}>\mathrm{XeF}_{4}>\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$.
Hence, the correct decreasing order of the given 'Xe' compound to act as both fluorinating and oxidising agent is $\mathrm{XeF}_{6}>\mathrm{XeF}_{4}>\mathrm{XeF}_{2}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation and Properties of Xenon – Fluorine Compounds

question:
Sulphur vapour is paramagnetic.
Reaction of dil. HCl with finely divided iron forms $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas.
The correct answer is
explanation:
Statement (A): Sulphur vapour is paramagnetic is correct statement as sulphur vapour has formula $\mathrm{S}_{2}$, i.e. similar to $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ and has two unpaired electrons in its anti-bonding molecular orbitals.
Statement (B): Reaction of HCl (dil.) with finely divided iron forms $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas is a wrong statement as the whole reaction is as follows:\begin{equation*}\mathrm{Fe}+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{dil} .) \rightarrow \mathrm{FeCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})\end{equation*}
The $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ produced will prevent the formation of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$
Hence, option (1) is the correct answer.

options.text: Statement  A  is correct, but  Statement  B is wrong.
Statement  A  is wrong, but  Statement  B  is correct.

question: Xenon hexafluoride reacts with silica to form a xenon compound ${\rm{X}}$. The oxidation state of xenon in ${\rm{X}}$ is
explanation:
Xenon hexafluoride reacts with silica to form $\mathrm{XeOF}_{4}$ as
$2 \mathrm{XeF}_{6}+\mathrm{SiO}_{2} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{XeOF}_{4}+\mathrm{SiF}_{4}$
The oxidation state of xenon in $\mathrm{XeOF}_{4}$ is +6 as calculated below:
Let the oxidation state of $\mathrm{Xe}$ is $a$.
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \quad & a+1 \times(-2)+4 \times(-1)=0 \\& a-2-4=0 \quad a=+6\end{aligned}$

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ is stronger acid than $\mathrm{PH}_{3}$


Reason: $\mathrm{S}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{P}$, conjugate base $\mathrm{HS}^{-}$is more stable than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{P}^{-}$.
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{aq} \rightarrow \mathrm{HS}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{\text {(aq) }}^{+}$
$\mathrm{PH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{aq} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{P}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}$
As $\mathrm{S}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{P}$, conjugate base $\mathrm{HS}^{-}$is more stable than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{P}^{-}$. Hence, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ is stronger acid than $\mathrm{PH}_{3}$. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Helium is used in diving apparatus.


Reason: Solubility of helium is less in blood.
explanation: In diving apparatus, a blend of oxygen and helium is employed to achieve an equivalent partial pressure of oxygen as that found in normal air at sea level $(1 \mathrm{~atm})$. Nitrogen has increased solubility in blood and can lead to the formation of bubbles, causing decompression sickness or "the bends." To mitigate this risk, nitrogen is substituted with helium, as it is significantly less soluble in biological fluids, reducing the likelihood of bubble formation during decompression. So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question: The fluoride ions make the enamel of teeth much harder by converting, which of the following substance/compound?

$\left[3 \mathrm{Ca}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \cdot \mathrm{Ca}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}\right]+2 \mathrm{~F}^{-}$ $ \to \mathop {\left[ {3{\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_3}{{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_4}} \right)}_2} \cdot {\text{Ca}}{{\text{F}}_2}} \right]}\limits_{{\text{Harder}}\,\,{\text{teeth}}\,\,{\text{enamel}}} $

question: Among Group-15 elements, what is the maximum covalency of an element having weakest $\mathrm{E}-\mathrm{E}$ bond (E = element) ?
explanation:
The ${\rm{E}} - {\rm{E}}$ bond energies of the elements of group-15 are
$\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{N} \quad 167 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{P} \quad 201 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
${\rm{As}} - {\rm{As}}$ $\quad 146 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
$\mathrm{Sb}-\mathrm{Sb} \quad 121 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Antimony (Sb) has the weakest ${\rm{E}} - {\rm{E}}$ bond energy and its maximum covalency is 5

question:
Identify the incorrect statements about group 15 elements.
(I) Dinitrogen is a diatomic gas which acts like an inert gas at room temperature.
(II) The common oxidation states of these elements are ${\mathrm{-3,+3}}$ and +5 .
(III) Nitrogen has unique ability to form ${\mathrm{\mathrm{p} \pi-\mathrm{p} \pi}}$ multiple bonds.
(IV) The stability of +5 oxidation state increases down the group.
(V) Nitrogen shows a maximum covalency of 6 .
Choose the correct answer from the options given below.
explanation:
The stability of +5 oxidation state decreases down the group 15 elements due to inert pair effect.
Nitrogen shows a maximum covalency of 4 because it can not expand its octet due to absence of d orbitals in its valance shell.
So, the correct option is (3).
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Miscellaneous Questions on Introduction to Group – 15 Elements

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below
${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ can act as ligands to form transition metal complexes.
As N and P are from same group, the nature of bonding of ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ is always same with transition metals.
explanation:
Both ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ can act as
${\mathrm{\sigma}}$ - donors due to presence of lone pair of electrons on central atom.
${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ can also act as ${\mathrm{\pi}}$-acceptor due to the availability of vacant d-orbitals in phosphorous while ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ does not due to non availability of vacant ${\mathrm{d}}$ - orbitals in nitrogen. Thus, nature of bonding in ${\mathrm{\mathrm{N}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ and ${\mathrm{\mathrm{P}\left(\mathrm{CH}_{3}\right)_{3}}}$ is not always same. So, the correct option is (1).

options.text: Statement A is correct but Statement B is incorrect.
Statement A is incorrect but Statement B is correct.

question: Which among the group- 15 elements does not exists as tetra atomic molecule?

Nitrogen due to smaller size, high ionisation energy high electronegativity and the absence of d-orbital does not exist as a tetra atomic molecule.
Nitrogen cannot form $\mathrm{d} \pi-\mathrm{p} \pi$ bond due to absence of d-orbital, so it cannot expand its covalency beyond four as the heavier members of this group can.

question:
Read the Statement - A and Statement - B carefully to mark the correct option given below
Oxygen being the first member of group 16 exhibits only -2 oxidation state.

Statement ${\mathrm{A}}$ is incorrect as along with $-$2 oxidation state, oxygen shows
${\mathrm{-1\left(\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{2}\right), 0\left(\mathrm{O}_{2}\right),-\dfrac{1}{2}\left(\mathrm{KO}_{2}\right),+1\left(\mathrm{O}_{2} \mathrm{~F}_{2}\right)}}$ and${\mathrm{+2\left(\mathrm{OF}_{2}\right)}}$ oxidation states.
Statement ${\mathrm{B}}$ is also incorrect as down the group stability of +4 oxidation state increases and +6 oxidation state decreases due to inert pair effect. So, the correct option is (4).

question:
Read the Assertion and Reason carefully to mark the correct option out of the options given below.

Assertion: Both rhombic and monoclinic sulphur exist as ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}_{8}}}$ while oxygen exists as ${\mathrm{\mathrm{O}_{2}}}$.

Reason: Oxygen forms ${\rm{p\pi }} - {\rm{p\pi }}$ multiple bonds with itself and other elements having small size and high electronegativity like C,N, which is not possible for sulphur.
explanation:
Due to small size and high electronegativity, oxygen forms ${\mathrm{\mathrm{p} \pi-\mathrm{p} \pi}}$ multiple bonds with itself and with other elements having similar size like carbon, nitrogen, etc. This tendency of forming ${\mathrm{\mathrm{p} \pi-\mathrm{p} \pi}}$ multiple bonds with itself is not shown by sulphur, due to larger size and lower electronegativity. But sulphur can form multiple bond with small size atoms like C and N .
e.g., ${\mathrm{\mathrm{S}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{S}, \mathrm{S}=\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{N}^{-}}}$.
So, the correct option is (2).

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but reason is correct.

Assertion: Dinegative anion of oxygen $\left(\mathrm{O}^{-2}\right)$ is quite common but dinegative anion of sulphur $\left(\mathrm{S}^{2-}\right)$ is less common.


Reason: Covalency of oxygen is two.
explanation: Oxygen ranks as the second most electronegative element, following fluorine. This characteristic allows oxygen to readily acquire electrons and form a doubly negative anion $\left(\mathrm{O}^{-2}\right)$, a trait not as prominent in sulfur, which is less electronegative compared to oxygen. Covalency of oxygen is 2 . So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Among chalcogens, tendency of catenation is maximum for sulphur.


Reason: $\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{S}$ bond dissociation energy is higher than $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bond dissociation energy.
explanation: Catenation is directly proportional to bond energy. Sulfur's strong catenation ability, second only to carbon, stems from its comparable S-S homoatomic bond energy to heteroatomic bond energies. This equivalence enables sulfur atoms to form robust bonds, facilitating the creation of extensive chains or rings. As S-S bond energy is more than $\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{O}$ bond energy. It's catenation is more than oxygen. Therefore, option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: The bond dissociation energy increases from $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ and then decreases to $\mathrm{I}_{2}$.

Reason: The low bond energy of fluorine is due to the repulsion between the lone pairs of electrons in two fluorine atoms.
The correct option among the following is
explanation: Both Assertion and Reason are true and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion. As bond length increases, bond dissociation enthalpy decreases. Hence, bond dissociation enthalpy decreases from $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ to $\mathrm{I}_{2}$. But the bond dissociation enthalpy of $\mathrm{F}_{2}$ is lower than $\mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{Br}_{2}$. It is due to the presence of lone pair of electron on fluorine atom which creates greater repulsion due to small size of fluorine. Hence, the bond dissociation enthalpy order is, $\mathrm{I}_{2} < \mathrm{F}_{2} < \mathrm{Br}_{2} < \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{F}$ bond has low bond dissociation energy.


Reason: The fluorine has lower reactivity.
explanation:
The lower bond dissociation energy in fluorine $(\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{F})$ is due to heightened interelectronic repulsions in its small-sized 2p-orbital. This makes $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{F}$ bond weaker compared to
$\mathrm{Cl}$ $-\mathrm{Cl}$ and $\mathrm{Br}-\mathrm{Br}$ bonds. Bond dissociation energies are: ${\text{F - F(38 k cal/mol),Cl - Cl(57 k cal/mol)}}$ ${\text{Br - Br (45}}{\text{.5 k cal/mol) ,}}$ and ${\text{I - I (35}}{\text{.6 k cal/mol)}}.$ ${{\text{F}}_2}$ is highly reactive non metal. So option (3) is correct.

question: Arrange the hydrides $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{HF}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{HCl}$ in the increasing order of their boiling points
explanation:
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{HF}$ and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ have hydrogen bonding as the intermolecular forces while HCl has weaker dipole dipole interaction.
Thus, HCl has the lowest boiling point. Among $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}, \mathrm{HF}$, and $\mathrm{NH}_{3}, \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ has the highest boiling point due to extensive network of hydrogen bonds. Among $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ and HF, HF forms stronger hydrogen bonds due to very high electronegativity of fluorine. Thus, the order of boiling point will be : $\mathrm{HCl} < \mathrm{NH}_{3} < \mathrm{HF} < \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

question:
Catalysts in the following reactions are
(I)$\mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOCH}_{3}(\mathrm{l})+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l}) \rightarrow \mathrm{CH}_{3} \mathrm{COOH}(\mathrm{aq})$
(II) $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
(III) $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+\mathrm{O}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
(IV) $\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
explanation:
I. Esterification reaction is catalysed by mineral acid such as $\mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{l})$.
II. Conversion of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ into $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ is catalysed by $\operatorname{Pt}(\mathrm{s})$.
III. In lead chamber process conversion of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ into $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ by reaction with $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is catalysed by $\mathrm{NO}(\mathrm{g})$.
IV. In Haber's process ammonia is manufactured by reacting $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ in presence of $\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{s})$.

question:
Sulphur vapour is paramagnetic.
Reaction of dil. HCl with finely divided iron forms $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas.
The correct answer is
explanation:
Statement (A): Sulphur vapour is paramagnetic is correct statement as sulphur vapour has formula $\mathrm{S}_{2}$, i.e. similar to $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ and has two unpaired electrons in its anti-bonding molecular orbitals.
Statement (B): Reaction of HCl (dil.) with finely divided iron forms $\mathrm{FeCl}_{3}$ and $\mathrm{H}_{2}$ gas is a wrong statement as the whole reaction is as follows:\begin{equation*}\mathrm{Fe}+2 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{dil} .) \rightarrow \mathrm{FeCl}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})\end{equation*}
The $\mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ produced will prevent the formation of $\mathrm{Fe}^{3+}$
Hence, option (1) is the correct answer.

Statement  A  is wrong, but  Statement  B  is correct.

$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{(\mathrm{g})}+\mathrm{aq} \rightarrow \mathrm{HS}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{\text {(aq) }}^{+}$
$\mathrm{PH}_{3(\mathrm{~g})}+\mathrm{aq} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{P}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{-}+\mathrm{H}_{(\mathrm{aq})}^{+}$
As $\mathrm{S}$ is more electronegative than $\mathrm{P}$, conjugate base $\mathrm{HS}^{-}$is more stable than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{P}^{-}$. Hence, $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ is stronger acid than $\mathrm{PH}_{3}$. So option (1) is correct.

question: What is the formula of the product formed when $\mathrm{F}^{-}$reacts with enamel on teeth?
explanation:
Enamel of teeth is $\mathrm{Ca}_{10}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{6}(\mathrm{OH})_{2}$, when it reacts with $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ions, following reaction will take place.
${\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_{10}}{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_4}} \right)_6}{({\text{OH}})_2} + 2\,{{\text{F}}^ - } \to $ ${\text{C}}{{\text{a}}_{10}}{\left( {{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_4}} \right)_6}\,{{\text{F}}_2} + 2{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }$
$\mathrm{Ca}_{10}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{6} \mathrm{~F}_{2}$ can be rewritten as: $3\left[\mathrm{Ca}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{CaF}_{2}$ or $3\left[\mathrm{Ca}_{3}\left(\mathrm{PO}_{4}\right)_{2} \cdot \mathrm{CaF}_{2}\right]$
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.

So (ii) and (iii) give dinitrogen.

question:
The total number of paramagnetic gaseous products formed in all the following reactions $[{\text{A}} + {\text{B}} + {\text{C}}]$ are
(A) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\Delta}$ Products
(B) $\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow$ Products
(C) $\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \xrightarrow{673 \mathrm{~K}}$ Products
explanation:
(A) The $\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}$ produced by heating
$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3}$ is a gas.
$\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\text { Heat }} \mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
It is diamagnetic as it does not have unpaired electron.
(B) No gaseous product is formed in reaction (B)
$2 \mathrm{NaNO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{2}+\mathrm{Na}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$
(C) $2 \mathrm{~Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \xrightarrow{673 \mathrm{~K}} 4 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{PbO}+\mathrm{O}_{2}$
Nitric oxide and oxygen are gases produced in this reaction. $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ is paramagnetic because it has two unpaired electrons. $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$ is paramagnetic as it has unpaired electron at nitrogen.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Miscellaneous Questions based on Nitrogen and Its Compounds

question: A nitrogen oxide that forms "in situ" when dilute $\mathrm{FeSO}_{4}$ is treated with aqueous solution of nitrate ion and then careful addition of conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ along the sides of test tube, is

The test for nitrate ion is called "brown ring test".

${\text{NO}} + {\left[ {{\text{Fe}}{{\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \right)}_6}} \right]^{2 - }} \to {\mathop {\left[ {{\text{Fe}}{{\left( {{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}} \right)}_5}({\text{NO}})} \right]}\limits_{{\text{ Brown ring }}} ^{2 - }} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$

Give the correct characteristics colours of the products (P,Q,R,S) formed in the following reactions.

(C) $\mathrm{Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2} \xrightarrow{673 \mathrm{~K}} \mathrm{R}+\mathrm{PbO}$

explanation:
(A) $\underset{\text { Nitric oxide }}{\mathrm{NO}}+\underset{\substack{\text { Nitrogen } \\ \text { dioxide }}}{\mathrm{NO}_{2}} \xrightarrow{250 \mathrm{~K}} \underset{\substack{\text { Dinitrogen trioxide } \\ \text { (Blue) }}}{\mathrm{N}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}(\mathrm{P})}$
(B) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952089/preptracker/questions/q_107181_explanation_1764952089212.png
(C) https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764952090/preptracker/questions/q_107181_explanation_1764952089866.png
(D) $\mathop {4{\text{HN}}{{\text{O}}_3}}\limits_{{\text{Nitric}}\,\,{\text{acid}}} + \mathop {{{\text{P}}_4}{{\text{O}}_{10}}}\limits_{{\text{Phosphorus}}\,\,{\text{pentoxide}}} \to $ $\mathop {2{{\rm{N}}_2}{{\rm{O}}_5}({\rm{S}})}\limits_{{\rm{Dinitrogen}}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} {\rm{pentoxide}}{\kern 1pt} {\kern 1pt} ({\rm{Colourless}})} + \mathop {4{\rm{HP}}{{\rm{O}}_3}}\limits_{{\rm{Phosphoric}}\,\,{\rm{acid}}} $
So, the correct option is (1).

question: For the formation of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$ from its constituent elements, the favourable conditions for its formation are
explanation:
Formation of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ takes place according to Le-Chatelier's principle.
$\mathrm{N}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_{3}(\mathrm{~g})$
Number of moles of reactants $=1+3=4$
Number of moles of product $=2$
As number of moles of product is less than number of moles of reactants, increase in pressure will favour forward direction.
As forward reaction is exothermic, decrease in temperature will favour forward direction. Hence, high pressure and low temperature are favourable conditions for formation of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$.
subject: Chemistry
subtopic: Preparation and Properties of Ammonia

NO is paramagnetic in the gaseous state and diamagnetic in liquid state.

NO is paramagnetic in gaseous state and diamagnetic in liquid-state as NO in liquid state exists as dimer with no unpaired electron in the structure. So, statement (B) is correct.

question:
Identify the reactions in which $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is liberated
(i) $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+\mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow$
(ii) $\mathrm{NH}_{3}+\mathrm{Cl}_{2} \rightarrow$
(iii) $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7} \xrightarrow{\Delta}$
(iv) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\Delta}$
(v) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow$
explanation:
(i) $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \rightarrow$ $2{\rm{N}}{{\rm{H}}_3} + 2{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{O}} + {\rm{N}}{{\rm{a}}_2}{\rm{S}}{{\rm{O}}_4}$
(ii) $8 \mathrm{NH}_{3}+3 \mathrm{Cl}_{2}$ (excess) $\rightarrow \mathrm{N}_{2}+6 \mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}$
(iii) $\left(\mathrm{NH}_{4}\right)_{2} \mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{~N}_{2}+\mathrm{Cr}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{3}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(iv) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{NO}_{3} \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{~N}_{2} \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
(v) $\mathrm{NH}_{4} \mathrm{Cl}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{NaNO}_{2}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow$ ${{\rm{N}}_2} + 2{{\rm{H}}_2}{\rm{O}} + {\rm{NaCl}}$
$\mathrm{N}_{2}$ is being liberated in equation (ii), (iii) and (v).

question:
Assertion: $\mathrm{AgCl}$ is more soluble in $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ than in water.


Reason: Ammonia is more polar than water.
explanation:
Solubility of $\mathrm{AgCl}\left(\right.$ at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ) in $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
$=0.0020 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ per litre of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$
Solubility of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ in $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ (at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ )
${\text{ = 14}}{\text{.00 g AgCl}}$ dissolved per $\mathrm{kg}$ of $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$
Solubility of $\mathrm{AgCl}$ is more in $\mathrm{NH}_{3}$ due to formation of soluble stable complex
$\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right]^{+}$.Ammonia is less polar than water, as oxygen is more electronegative than nitrogen. So option (3) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect.
Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: Nitrogen is unreactive at room temperature but becomes reactive at elevated temperatures or in presence of a catalyst.


Reason: In nitrogen molecule, there is extensive delocalization of electrons.
explanation: The high dissociation energy of nitrogen, measured at $946 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$, coupled with its small size, accounts for its inert nature or lack of reactivity. There is no delocalisation of electrons in $\mathrm{N}_{2}$ molecule. So option (3) is correct.

Reason: In $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ there are two nitrogen oxygen bonds while in $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$ it is only one.
explanation: https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954271/preptracker/questions/q_25866_explanation_1764954270985.png  The acid strength of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ is higher than that of $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$ due to the higher oxidation state of nitrogen ( +5 in $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ compared to +3 in $\mathrm{HNO}_{2}$ ). Therefore, option (3) is correct.

question: Which of the following products will be obtained when copper metal is reacted with $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ ?
explanation:
The products of copper reaction with $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ depends upon the concentration of $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ used.
Copper metal reacts with dilute $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ to form nitrogen (II) oxide (NO).
$\begin{aligned}& 3 \mathrm{Cu}+8 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}(\text { dil. }) \rightarrow \\& 3 \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO}+4 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$
Copper metal reacts with conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ to form nitrogen (IV) oxide or nitrogen dioxide $\left(\mathrm{NO}_{2}\right)$.$\mathrm{Cu}+4 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}$ (conc.) $\rightarrow \mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NO}_{3}\right)_{2}+2 \mathrm{NO}_{2}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

$\mathrm{HNO}_{3}+3 \mathrm{HCl} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{NOCl}+2[\mathrm{Cl}]$

question: When nitric acid reacts with nitric oxide, a gas is released, which converts $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}$ into
explanation:

$\begin{aligned}& 2 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}+\mathrm{NO} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+3 \mathrm{NO}_{2} \\& \mathrm{NO}_{2}+\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S} \rightarrow \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{S}+\mathrm{NO}\end{aligned}$

question: Which of the following is obtained by catalytic oxidation of ammonia?
explanation:
Nitric oxide (NO) is obtained by catalytic oxidation of ammonia. The catalytic oxidation of ammonia uses a metal catalyst such as platinum, copper or nickel. This metal catalyst is heated, so that ammonia can reduce it, oxygen added to the system can then oxidise the ammonia, forming nitric oxide.
${\text{4N}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{3}}}{\text{ + 5}}{{\text{O}}_{\text{2}}}\xrightarrow[{{\text{800}}\,{\text{ - }}\,{\text{90}}{{\text{0}}^{\text{o}}}\,{\text{C}}}]{{{\text{Pt}}\,\,{\text{guage}}}}{\text{4NO}}\,\,{\text{ + }}\,\,{\text{6}}{{\text{H}}_{\text{2}}}{\text{O;}}\,\,{\text{ + }}\,\,{\text{21}}{\text{.5 K}}\,\,{\text{cal}}{\text{.}}$

question: Which one of the following properties is not shown by NO?
explanation:
The electronic configuration of ${\rm{NO}}\left[ {{\rm{15}}{{\rm{e}}^{\rm{ - }}}} \right]$ is given by
${\rm{\sigma 1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,}}{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{1}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,\sigma 2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,}}{{\rm{\sigma }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2}}{{\rm{s}}^{\rm{2}}}{\rm{,\sigma 2p}}_{\rm{z}}^{\rm{2}}{\rm{,\pi 2p}}_{\rm{x}}^{\rm{2}}{\rm{ = \pi 2p}}_{\rm{y}}^{\rm{2}}{\rm{,}}{{\rm{\pi }}^{\rm{*}}}{\rm{2p}}_{\rm{z}}^{\rm{1}}$
Thus, NO possess one unpaired electron hence it is paramagnetic in nature. However, it dimerises at low temperature to become diamagnetic.
${\rm{2NO}} \to {{\rm{N}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}$
Bond order of ${\rm{NO = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{[10 - 5] = 2}}{\rm{.5}}$
Thus, bond order of NO is 2.5 and it combines with $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ to give $\mathrm{NO}_{2}$.

It combines with oxygen to form nitrogen dioxide

question:
$\mathrm{P}_{4}+3 \mathrm{NaOH}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CO}_{2}} 3 \mathrm{X}+\mathrm{PH}_{3} \uparrow$

explanation:
Statement given in option (2) is incorrect, because '${\text{Y}}$' is hypophosphorus acid.
$\mathrm{P}_{4}+3 \mathrm{NaOH}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{CO}_{2}} \underset{(\mathrm{X})}{3 \mathrm{NaH}_{2}} \mathrm{PO}_{2}+\mathrm{PH}_{3}$

$\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{2}$ is called hypophosphorus acid and phosphinic acid. It contains has only one ionisable (H) ion, hence its basicity is 1 and is a reducing agent due to $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{H}$ bonds. Oxidation state of ' P ' is +1 in $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{2}$.

Phosphoric acid gives three $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ ions thus, it is tribasic.

question: Match the following:
explanation:
A - Q; When white ${{\text{P}}_4}$ reacts with hydroxide ion in aqueous medium then hypophosphorus acid $\left(\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{2}\right)$ is obtained.
${{\text{P}}_4} + 4{\text{O}}{{\text{H}}^ - }\xrightarrow{{4{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} + {{\text{H}}^ + }}}4{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_2}$
B - R; When phosphorus acid ($\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{3}$) reacts with $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ in aqueous solution then pyrophosphorus acid is formed.
${{\text{H}}_3}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_3} + {\text{PC}}{{\text{l}}_3} + 2{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to {{\text{H}}_4}{{\text{P}}_2}{{\text{O}}_5} + 3{\text{HCl}}$
$\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{S} ; \mathrm{P}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{5}$ (phosphorus pentoxide) in aqueous medium, gives phosphoric acid.
${{\text{P}}_2}{{\text{O}}_5} + 3{{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}} \to \mathop {2{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_4}}\limits_{{\text{ Phosphoric acid }}} $
D - T; Pyrophosphoric acid is obtained from thermodynamic reaction of $\mathrm{H}_{3} \mathrm{PO}_{4}$.
$2{{\text{H}}_3}{\text{P}}{{\text{O}}_4}\xrightarrow{\Delta }{{\text{H}}_4}{{\text{P}}_2}{{\text{O}}_7} + {{\text{H}}_2}{\text{O}}$
Thus, option (4) is correct.

Assertion: Bond lengths of $\mathrm{P}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bonds in gaseous $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ and solid $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ are not equal.


Reason: Because in solid state two $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ molecules are associated.
explanation: In the solid state, $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ forms an ionic solid $\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{4}\right]^{+}\left[\mathrm{PCl}_{6}\right]^{-}$with tetrahedral and octahedral structures, respectively. In the gaseous state, $\mathrm{PCl}_{5}$ adopts a trigonal bipyramidal shape, in which equatorial bonds are equivalent and axial bonds are longer resulting from increased repulsion between additional bond pairs. So option (1) is correct.

question:
Assertion: White phosphorus is stored under water.


Reason: White phosphorus is highly reactive and catches fire spontaneously in air.
explanation: White phosphorus has a notably low ignition temperature, approximately around $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. When exposed to air, it easily ignites, producing dense fumes of phosphorus pentoxide. To prevent this reaction, white phosphorus is typically stored under water, avoiding its contact with atmospheric oxygen and minimizing the risk of spontaneous combustion. So option (1) is correct.

question: Formation of $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$ is explained on the basis of what hybridisation of phosphorus atom?
explanation:
The type of hybridisation in an atom can be determined by using the formula
$\mathrm{H}=\dfrac{1}{2}[\mathrm{~V}+\mathrm{Y}-\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{A}]$
Here, $V=$ valence shell electrons of the central atom
$\mathrm{Y}=$ number of monovalent atom
$\mathrm{C}=$ total positive charge
and $\mathrm{A}=$ total negative charge
In case of $\mathrm{PCl}_{3}$,
$\mathrm{H}=\dfrac{1}{2}[5+3]=4$
$\therefore$ Hybridisation of phosphorus atom is $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953135/preptracker/questions/q_25285_explanation_1764953134301.png Pyramidal shape (due to presence of one lone pair of electron.)

$\left.\left.\begin{array}{l}\mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+4 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{4}\right] \mathrm{SO}_{4} \\\mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+2 \mathrm{NH}_{3} \longrightarrow\left[\mathrm{Ag}\left(\mathrm{NH}_{3}\right)_{2}\right] \mathrm{NO}_{3}\end{array}\right\} \begin{array}{l}\text { Complex } \\\text { formation } \\3 \mathrm{CuSO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{PH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}_{3} \mathrm{P}_{2}+3 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4} \\3 \mathrm{AgNO}_{3}+\mathrm{PH}_{3} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}_{3} \mathrm{P}+3 \mathrm{HNO}_{3}\end{array}\right\} \text { Precipitation }$
So, the (3) is correct.

1 mol of white phosphorus produces $12 \mathrm{~mol}$ of $\mathrm{HCl}$
$62 \mathrm{~g}$ of white phosphorus has been taken which is equivalent to $\frac{{{\rm{62}}}}{{{\rm{124}}}}{\rm{ = }}\frac{{\rm{1}}}{{\rm{2}}}{\rm{\,mol}}$.
Therefore $6 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{HCl}$ will be formed
Mass of $6 \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{HCl}=6 \times 36.5=219.0 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{HCl}$.

explanation:

question: The final acid product obtained during the synthesis of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ by contact process is
explanation:
The final acid product obtained during the synthesis of $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ by contact process is $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{~S}_{2} \mathrm{O}_{7}$, i.e. oleum.
The following reaction(s) are involved in the formation of oleum.
(i) $2 \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\mathrm{O}_{2} \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{SO}_{3}$
(ii) ${{\text{H}}_2}{\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_4} + {\text{S}}{{\text{O}}_3} \to \mathop {{{\text{H}}_2}\,{{\text{S}}_2}{{\text{O}}_7}}\limits_{{\text{Oleum}}} $
Hence, option (4) is the correct answer.

question:
Assertion: In $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$, the bond angle is $119^{\circ}$ whereas in $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$, the bond angle is $120^{\circ}$.


Reason: $\mathrm{S}$ atom in both $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ is $\mathrm{sp}^{2}-$ hybridized.
explanation: $\mathrm{S}$ atom in both $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and $\mathrm{SO}_{3}$ is $\mathrm{sp}^{2}$ hybridized. But it contains a lone-pair of electrons in $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ which results in bent shape with $119^{\circ}$ bond angle. So option (2) is correct.

question:
Assertion: Caro's acid has ${\text{S}}$ atom in +6 oxidation state.

Reason: Caro's acid contains one peroxo $\mathrm{O}_{2}^{2-}$ group.
explanation:
This can be explained through structure of caro's acid (peroxomonosulphuric acid). https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764954284/preptracker/questions/q_25924_explanation_1764954283820.png Oxidation no. of $\mathrm{S}=\mathrm{x}$, oxidation no. of
$\mathrm{H}=+1$, Oxidation no. of $\mathrm{O}$ in peroxo
linkage $=-1$ (each),
Oxidation no. of other oxygen
atoms $=-2$ (each).
$2+\mathrm{x}-6-2=0$ or
$\mathrm{x}=+6$
So option (1) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Assertion is incorrect but Reason is correct.

question:
Assertion: In ${{\text{S}}_{\text{8}}}$ molecules, each ${\text{S}}$ atom is bonded to two $\mathrm{S}$ atoms.


Reason: Each $\mathrm{S}$ atom in $\mathrm{S}_{8}$ molecules is $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridized.
explanation: In a sulfur molecule $\left( {{{\text{S}}_{\text{8}}}} \right)$, catenation leads to each sulfur atom forming covalent bonds with two neighbours, creating a stable and symmetric octatomic sulfur structure. After forming two bond pairs it remains left with two lone pairs . So 2 bond pair +2 lone pairs $=4$ orbitals required i.e $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$. Therefore, option (2) is correct.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

question: On addition of conc. $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ to a chloride salt, colourless fumes are evolved but in case of iodide salt, violet fumes come out. This is because:
explanation:
Hydrogen iodide (HI) is more stronger oxidising agent than $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$. So, it reduces $\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}$ to $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ and itself oxidises to $\mathrm{I}_{2}$.
$\mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{SO}_{4}+2 \mathrm{HI} \rightarrow \mathrm{SO}_{2}+\underset{\substack{\text { Violet } \\ \text { colour }}}{\mathrm{I}_{2}}+2 \mathrm{H}_{2} \mathrm{O}$

question:
Assertion: The $\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{S}-\mathrm{S}$ bond angle in $\mathrm{S}_{8}$ molecule is $107^{\circ}$.


Reason: $\mathrm{S}_{8}$ has V - shape.
explanation: $\mathrm{S}$ - atoms in $\mathrm{S}_{8}$ molecule are $\mathrm{sp}^{3}$ hybridised and contain two lone pairs of electrons on each sulphur atom and exist as staggered eight atom ring. Tetrahedral angle is $109^{\circ}$ but due to lone pairof electrons, it reduces to $107^{\circ} . \mathrm{S}_{8}$ ring is puckered and has a crown shape, but each ' $\mathrm{S}$ ' atom is in V-shape only.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct and Reason is the correct explanation of the Assertion.
Both Assertion and Reason are correct but Reason is not the correct explanation of the Assertion.

Both Assertion and Reason are incorrect.

question: Which is the most stable allotrope of sulphur?

Its crystal structure contains cyclic $\mathrm{S}_{8}$ rings packed in a manner which leads to the formation of Rhombic Crystals. https://res.cloudinary.com/doe6oiv2h/image/upload/v1764953161/preptracker/questions/q_25308_explanation_1764953160570.png (Rhombic or $\alpha$ or octahedral sulphur)

question:
Two methods of preparation of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ are given below.
I. ${\rm{4Fe}}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(s) + 11}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}} \to {\rm{2F}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}{\rm{(s) + 8S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$
II.${\rm{SO}}_{\rm{3}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}{\rm{(aq) + 2}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}{\rm{(aq)}} \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O(l) + S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{(g)}}$
Categorise these reactions into laboratory method and industrial method of preparing $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ with the help of options given below:
explanation:
Industrial method of preparation of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ is
${\rm{4Fe}}{{\rm{S}}_{\rm{2}}}\left( {\rm{s}} \right){\rm{ + 11}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}\left( {\rm{g}} \right) \to {\rm{2F}}{{\rm{e}}_{\rm{2}}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{3}}}\left( {\rm{s}} \right){\rm{ + 8S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}\left( {\rm{g}} \right)$
Laboratory method of preparation of $\mathrm{SO}_{2}$ is
${\rm{SO}}_{\rm{3}}^{{\rm{2 - }}}\left( {{\rm{aq}}} \right){\rm{ + 2}}{{\rm{H}}^{\rm{ + }}}\left( {{\rm{aq}}} \right) \to {{\rm{H}}_{\rm{2}}}{\rm{O}}\left( {\rm{l}} \right){\rm{ + S}}{{\rm{O}}_{\rm{2}}}\left( {\rm{g}} \right){\rm{.}}$

question: Which form of sulphur shows paramagnetic behaviour?
explanation: In vapour state sulphur partly exists as $\mathrm{S}_{2}$ molecule which has two unpaired electrons in the antibonding $\pi^{*}$ orbitals like $\mathrm{O}_{2}$ and hence exhibits paramagnetism.
